t: — Biofeedback, manual therapy, dilator therapy (gradual size increase).
— Fear of pain and avoidance; ter Kuile M.M. RCT.
Topical anesthetic (lidocaine) — in provoked vulvodynia (Goldstein A.T.).
Botulinum toxin A — in refractory vaginismus; evidence base is growing.
ter Kuile M.M. et al. CBT vaginismus.
Goldstein A.T. et al. ISSWSH vulvodyniya konsensus.
Gradual increase in size; combined with relaxation.
With a multidisciplinary approach, prognosis is good (70–90%); in refractory cases, botox or intensive psychological intervention.
Myth 1: "Vaginismus is an emotional rejection of the partner"
Reflex muscle tension — outside conscious control; accusing harmful.
CBT + dilator + physiotherapy combination 70–90% efficacy; ter Kuile RCTs.
Myth 3: "Treatment should be done to increase penetration frequency"
target is reducing patient distress, restoring sense of control and choice; not a performance indicator.
Myth 4: "Local anesthetic is effective for all sexual pain"
Effective in provoked vulvodynia; use limited in generalized vaginismus — main problem is muscle tension.
Myth 5: "Forcing 'compulsory exercise' helps with penetration attempts"
urgent, painful repetition increases avoidance and fear; gradual, controlled exposure effective.
WHO. ICD-11. HA04 Genito-pelvic pain/penetration disorder. 2024.
Ter Kuile, M.M. et al. Therapist-aided exposure for women with lifelong vaginismus: a randomized controlled trial. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 2013;81(6):1127–1136.
Goldstein A.T. et al. Vulvodynia: Assessment and Treatment. J Sex Med 2016;13(4):572–590.
Bergeron S. et al. Genital pain in women and men: It can hurt more than your sex life. Sex Med Rev 2020.
